why: Investing term of art for one's collection of holdings; the literal 'briefcase/folder' sense is wrong, and निवेश संग्रह sounds bookish, so English is the natural spoken form.
As an investing instruction it is spoken in English; a literal 'make varied' verb sounds clinical and is not how advisors actually say it.
चक्रवृद्धि (ब्याज) is the standard, genuinely-used native term for compound growth; Tamil கூட்டு வட்டி for compound interest is also natural.
In markets it means shares/ownership stake; the dictionary word for 'fairness/justice' (समता/சமத்துவம்) would be absurd, so the spoken form is English.
Fixed product name; a literal 'mutual collection' (पारस्परिक निधि) is unrecognizable to listeners who only know it as 'mutual fund'.
लाभांश (share of profit) and பங்காதாயம் are real, used native terms; preferred over the transliteration डिविडेंड.
Financial 'ease of converting to cash'; the literal 'liquid-ness' (तरलता/நீர்மை) reads as physics, not finance, so keep English.
महंगाई and விலைவாசி உயர்வு / பணவீக்கம் are the genuine everyday words for rising prices that ordinary people actually use.
मंदी is the natural, common Hindi word for an economic downturn; Tamil மந்தநிலை is the standard native term.
Business term for a company's assessed worth; native मूल्यांकन/மதிப்பீடு mean general 'assessment' and miss the deal/market sense, so English is spoken.
राजस्व and வருவாய் are real, natural native words for income/turnover used in business and government speech.
मुनाफ़ा/लाभ and லாபம் are the genuine, everyday native words for profit, used by shopkeepers and businesses alike.
देनदारी (what is owed) is the natural accounting term and கடன் பொறுப்பு works in Tamil; both are genuinely spoken, unlike the transliteration.
संपत्ति and சொத்து are the natural, ubiquitous native words for an asset/property in both languages.
Modern home-loan product spoken as 'mortgage'; बंधक/அடமானம் exist but read as archaic legal pledges, not today's product.
Everyday loanword for an insurance/payment amount; both languages use 'premium' naturally in spoken finance talk.
किस्त and தவணை are deeply rooted everyday native words for a periodic payment, used constantly in commerce.
ब्याज/வட்டி are the true native words for interest; 'rate' adds दर/விகிதம், both natural and widely spoken.
Fixed product/metric name (CIBIL-style); any literal translation is unintelligible, so it is always said in English.
गिरवी (pledged security) and பிணையம்/அடகு are genuine native words for loan collateral, more natural than the transliteration.
Risk-offsetting investing term; the literal 'fence/bush' (बाड़/வேலி) would be absurd, so keep English.
Borrowing-to-amplify-returns concept; literal 'lever force' (उत्तोलन/நெம்புகோல்) translations miss the financial meaning entirely.
उतार-चढ़ाव (ups and downs) and ஏற்ற இறக்கம் are the natural, widely-spoken market terms for price swings.
Reference standard/index term spoken in English; literal मानक/அளவுகோல் shift to 'standard/yardstick' and lose the index sense.
The trading-fee and broking-firm sense is said as 'brokerage'; native दलाली/தரகு carry a negative haggling connotation, so keep English.
Accounting term for asset value decline; native मूल्यह्रास/தேய்மானம் exist but are technical and rarely spoken aloud, so English is natural.
Loan-payoff schedule term; native ऋण परिशोधन/கடன் தணிப்பு are obscure formal coinages, so the English term is what is actually used.
'Budget' is a fully naturalized everyday loanword in both languages, used for household and government planning alike.
बचत and சேமிப்பு are the genuine, common native words people use for money set aside.
व्यय/खर्च and செலவு are natural, everyday native words for spending; no need for an English transliteration.
Fixed product name; a literal 'list fund' is meaningless, so it is always said in English.
Universally spoken acronym (Equated Monthly Installment); expanding or translating it would confuse listeners who only know 'EMI'.
'Loan' is the everyday spoken word in both languages; native कर्ज़/கடன் also exist but 'loan' is the common conversational form.
'Market' is a fully naturalized everyday loanword; both languages also have बाज़ार/சந்தை, but 'market' is freely used in finance speech.
In the investing 'funds/principal' sense it is spoken as 'capital'; पूंजी/மூலதனம் exist but the English term is standard in market talk.
Loan-non-payment sense is said as 'default'; native words for failure don't carry the precise credit meaning, so keep English.
बीमा and காப்பீடு are well-established, everyday native words for insurance used by everyone, not just specialists.
'Share' is the dominant everyday word for equity ownership in both languages; Tamil பங்கு is native, but 'share' is freely used in speech.
शेयर बाज़ार and பங்குச் சந்தை are the standard, natural localized forms; far more common than transliterating 'stock market'.
कर and வரி are fully native and standard for tax; the टैक्स loanword is also common, but the native words are universally understood.
